items.subtopic: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE
items.title: Understanding the Prevalence and Impact of Autoimmune Diseases (ADs)
items.content: Autoimmune diseases are chronic conditions where the immune system mistakenly attacks self-tissues. They significantly affect quality of life and can lead to increased mortality rates. The prevalence in the U.S. was estimated at 7.6–9.4% as of 2009, affecting approximately 25–31 million people.
keyPoints: Autoimmune diseases are defined by harmful adaptive immune responses against self-antigens.
Approximately 150 autoimmune diseases have been described.
Prevalence in the U.S. was estimated at 7.6–9.4% of the population (~25–31 million people) as of 2009.
Autoimmune diseases are among the top 10 leading causes of death in women.
Individuals with one autoimmune disease have a higher risk of developing additional autoimmune conditions and certain cancers.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE
items.title: Gender Disparities in the Incidence of Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: Autoimmune diseases predominantly affect females and are a leading cause of death among women. The risk is compounded by familial tendencies and associations with other health conditions like certain cancers and cardiovascular disease.
keyPoints: Many autoimmune diseases disproportionately affect females.
ADs rank in the top 10 causes of death for women.
Individuals with AD have a higher likelihood of developing additional autoimmune conditions.
There is an increased risk of certain cancers and premature cardiovascular disease among those with autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE

items.title: Genetic Susceptibility and Concordance Rates in Autoimmune Diseases (ADs)
items.content: Autoimmune diseases are influenced by genetic factors, with a notable familial aggregation. The concordance rates for ADs such as T1DM, RA, MS, and SLE in monozygotic twins range from 15 to 30%, while it is less than 5% in dizygotic twins. This indicates that both genetic predisposition and epigenetic factors contribute significantly to the risk of developing autoimmune diseases.
keyPoints: Concordance rates for ADs: 15-30% in monozygotic twins, <5% in dizygotic twins.
Familial aggregation is evident in ADs like T1DM, RA, MS, and SLE.
Genetic factors are crucial but not solely responsible; epigenetics also plays a role.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · GENETIC AND EPIGENETIC FACTORS

items.title: Role of HLA and Other Genetic Factors in Autoimmunity Risk
items.content: Specific human leukocyte antigen (HLA) types, particularly HLA haplotypes, are strong predictors of autoimmune disease risk. These genetic markers can influence the development of autoantibodies in family members of AD patients. Additionally, genes involved in immune regulation, such as those affecting B- and T-cell function and the type I interferon pathway, are associated with increased susceptibility to multiple autoimmune diseases.
keyPoints: HLA haplotype is a key predictor of AD risk.
Genes regulating immune responses (e.g., B- and T-cell function) enhance autoimmunity risk.
Overlap in gene variants across different ADs suggests common genetic pathways.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · GENETIC AND EPIGENETIC FACTORS

items.title: Environmental Triggers in Autoimmune Disease (AD) Development
items.content: Various environmental exposures contribute to the development of clinically relevant autoimmune diseases. These include ultraviolet light, infections, mucosal dysbiosis, tobacco use, exposure to silica, solvents, pesticides, mercury, certain dietary factors, medications, and recreational drugs. The mechanisms involve synthesis of reactive oxygen species (ROS), induction of proinflammatory pathways leading to DNA damage, lipid peroxidation causing protein modification, and neoantigen formation.
keyPoints: Environmental exposures linked to AD risk include ultraviolet light, infections, mucosal dysbiosis, tobacco use, silica, solvents, pesticides, mercury, dietary factors, medications, recreational drugs.
Mechanisms: synthesis of ROS, proinflammatory pathways causing DNA damage and lipid peroxidation leading to protein modification, neoantigen formation.
These mechanisms promote activation of innate and adaptive immune responses contributing to AD development.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS

items.title: Specific Environmental Risk Factors for Certain Autoimmune Diseases (ADs)
items.content: Cigarette smoking increases the risk for autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis (RA), Graves' disease, systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and multiple sclerosis (MS). It promotes citrullination and carbamylation of proteins in the lung, which can lead to disease development in genetically predisposed individuals. Silica exposure is associated with SLE, while solvents are linked to systemic sclerosis.
keyPoints: Cigarette smoking is a risk factor for RA, Graves' disease, SLE, and MS.
Smoking promotes citrullination and carbamylation of proteins in the lung.
Silica exposure associated with SLE; solvents linked to systemic sclerosis.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS

items.title: Sex Differences in Autoimmunity: Why Women Are More Affected?
items.content: Autoimmune diseases (ADs) are more prevalent in women, influenced by sex hormones, X-chromosome dosing, and immune-related genes. Estrogen enhances immune responses, while androgens suppress autoantibody synthesis in conditions like SLE. The presence of two X chromosomes increases the risk due to enhanced expression of immunity-related genes such as TLR-7, which is linked to type I IFN production.
keyPoints: Enhanced incidence and prevalence of ADs in women
Estrogen enhances Ag-specific responses and Ab production
Androgens suppress autoAb synthesis in SLE
Two X chromosomes increase risk due to TLR-7, TLR-8, IRAK-1 expression
Breakdown in X chromosome inactivation linked to autoimmunity
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ☐ SEX | AGING

items.title: Aging and Autoimmunity: How Inflammaging Contributes?
items.content: Immune aging, or inflammaging, is characterized by the accumulation of senescent immune cells with proinflammatory features. This process can promote autoimmune diseases (ADs) like RA and giant cell arteritis in predisposed individuals. Factors such as genomic instability, mitochondrial dysfunction, and inefficient clearance of intracellular debris contribute to aberrant T-cell and myeloid cell dysfunction.
keyPoints: Immune aging leads to inflammaging
Inflammaging may promote overt AD in predisposed subjects
Genomic instability and mitochondrial dysfunction are involved
Inefficient clearance of cellular debris contributes to immune dysfunction
Linked to autoimmune diseases like RA and giant cell arteritis
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ☐ SEX | AGING

items.subtopic: ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE
items.title: How Dysregulated Cell Death Promotes Autoimmunity
items.content: Dysregulated or proinflammatory cell death, along with impaired clearance of dead cell debris, can lead to the generation of autoantigens (autoAgs) and induction of autoimmune responses. This is particularly relevant in genetically predisposed individuals where mechanisms that usually contain self-nucleic acid response are impaired.
keyPoints: Certain TLRs (7–9) may not distinguish between self- and microorganism-derived nucleic acids, leading to autoimmunity.
In genetically predisposed individuals, tolerance break can occur due to impaired mechanisms that usually contain responses to self-nucleic acids.
Delayed clearance of dead cells promotes secondary necrosis, releasing proinflammatory components and nucleic acids into the extracellular space.
Abnormalities in receptors or ligands for cell death clearance are linked to increased predisposition to SLE and other autoimmune diseases (ADs).
High frequency of SLE is observed in patients with deficiencies in complement pathway components, associated with impaired clearance of dying cells.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE

items.subtopic: ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE
items.title: Role of Cell Death Types in Autoimmune Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Different forms of cell death such as pyroptosis, necroptosis, and NETosis are associated with autoimmune and chronic inflammatory conditions. These processes can lead to the externalization and modification of intracellular autoantigens, promoting immune dysregulation.
keyPoints: Dysregulation of cell death types like pyroptosis, necroptosis, and NETosis is linked to autoimmune diseases.
Oxidation of nucleic acids during NETosis can enhance the half-life of modified RNA and DNA, preventing their degradation.
Impaired clearance mechanisms contribute to prolonged exposure of modified autoantigens, promoting immune dysregulation.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.title: Role of Type I IFNs in Autoimmune Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Type I interferons (IFNs) are implicated in the pathogenesis of several autoimmune diseases (ADs), including systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), primary Sjögren syndrome, and idiopathic inflammatory myopathies (IIMs). Chronic exposure or enhanced cellular response to type I IFNs can promote immune dysregulation features.
keyPoints: Type I IFNs are involved in antiviral defense but contribute to immune dysregulation in ADs.
Dysregulation is driven by genetic, epigenetic, and environmental factors.
Implicated diseases include SLE, primary Sjögren syndrome, and IIMs.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.title: Neutrophil Dysregulation in Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: Neutrophils, particularly low-density granulocytes (LDGs), and their dysregulated cell death mechanisms, such as neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs), play roles in the pathogenesis of autoimmune diseases by promoting inflammation and organ damage.
keyPoints: Neutrophils are first responders implicated in AD initiation and perpetuation.
Dysregulated NET formation leads to externalization of modified autoantigens.
Associated with SLE, AAV, IIM, primary APS, and RA.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.title: Trained Immunity and Autoimmunity
items.content: Trained immunity describes a phenomenon where innate immune responses become faster and more robust upon re-exposure to stimuli, potentially leading to aberrant inflammation and autoimmunity due to epigenetic changes.
keyPoints: Trained immunity involves epigenetic modifications like histone acetylation and DNA hypomethylation.
Can be beneficial against infections but may cause maladaptive inflammation in ADs.
Changes can occur in hematopoietic cells or tissue-resident cells.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.title: Mechanisms of Self-Tolerance and Autoimmunity
items.content: Self-tolerance is maintained through central and peripheral tolerance mechanisms. Central tolerance involves the deletion of autoreactive T cells in the thymus and high-affinity autoreactive B cells in the bone marrow. Peripheral tolerance includes lymphocyte anergy, cell death, regulatory lymphocyte suppression, inhibitory costimulatory signals, idiotypic network interactions, cytokine switching, competition for antigen among lymphocytes, and decreased frequency of lymphocyte precursors.
keyPoints: Central tolerance: Deletion of autoreactive T cells in the thymus and B cells in the bone marrow.

Peripheral tolerance mechanisms include anergy, cell death, regulatory suppression, inhibitory signals, idiotypic interactions, cytokine switching, competition for antigen, and decreased lymphocyte precursor frequency.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.title: Role of T Lymphocytes in Autoimmunity
items.content: T lymphocytes contribute to autoimmunity through direct cytotoxicity, proinflammatory cytokine release, reactive species production, and B-cell dysregulation. Dysregulation in T-cell pathways, including those involving PD-1, CTLA-4, and various interleukins (IL-2, IL-6, IL-17, IL-33), can lead to autoimmune responses. Polymorphisms in genes regulating T-cell function are linked to autoimmunity risk.
keyPoints: T cells contribute via cytotoxicity, cytokines, reactive species, and B-cell dysregulation.
Balance between activating/costimulatory pathways and inhibitory receptors (PD-1, CTLA-4) is crucial.
Dysregulation in T-cell pathways can lead to autoimmunity.
Polymorphisms in T-cell regulatory genes are associated with autoimmunity risk.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.title: B Cells and Autoantibodies in Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: Natural autoantibodies, primarily IgM, are present in healthy individuals and play a role in infection defense and immune homeostasis. Pathogenic high-affinity IgG autoantibodies arise through somatic hypermutation and class-switch recombination, disrupting cellular functions and signaling pathways. Autoantibodies are crucial for diagnosing autoimmune diseases and assessing disease severity.
keyPoints: Natural autoantibodies (IgM) are present in healthy individuals and aid in infection defense.
Pathogenic IgG autoantibodies arise through somatic hypermutation and class-switch recombination.
Autoantibodies disrupt cellular functions, signaling pathways, and can activate inflammatory cascades.
Autoantibodies are used for diagnosis and assessing disease severity in autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.title: Autoantibodies in Thyroid Disorders
items.content: In Graves' disease, autoantibodies target the TSH receptor on thyroid cells, enhancing thyroid hormone synthesis. In Hashimoto's thyroiditis, autoantibodies to thyroid peroxidase and thyroglobulin lead to complement fixation and a proinflammatory response.
keyPoints: Graves' disease: AutoAbs against TSH receptor enhance thyroid hormone synthesis.
Hashimoto's thyroiditis: AutoAbs target thyroid peroxidase and thyroglobulin, causing inflammation and cytotoxic injury.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.title: Autoantibodies in Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
items.content: Type 1 diabetes mellitus involves autoantibodies targeting beta-cell antigens, leading to pancreatic cell destruction. Detectable islet-antigen-specific antibodies can precede clinical disease by years.
keyPoints: T1DM: AutoAbs target beta-cell antigens like insulin and glutamic acid decarboxylase.
Islet-antigen-specific Abs detectable years before T1DM onset.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.title: Autoantibodies in Rheumatoid Arthritis
items.content: Rheumatoid arthritis is characterized by autoantibodies such as ACPAs and rheumatoid factor, which are associated with worse prognosis. ACPAs can be detected up to a decade before diagnosis.
keyPoints: RA: AutoAbs include ACPAs and rheumatoid factor.
ACPAs specific for RA, detectable years prior to clinical diagnosis.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.title: Role of Cytokines in Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: Inflammatory cytokines such as TNF, IL-1b, IL-6, IL-17, and type I IFNs play a crucial role in the development and maintenance of autoimmune diseases (ADs). Therapeutic advances have been made by targeting these cytokines.
keyPoints: Blocking TNF is highly effective in significant percentages of patients with RA, psoriasis, seronegative spondyloarthropathies, and IBD.
Type I IFNs are major players in autoimmunity; strategies to downregulate their signaling pathways include JAK inhibitors.
JAK inhibitors are used in ADs such as RA, psoriatic arthritis, IBD, SLE, primary Sjögren syndrome, vitiligo, and alopecia areata.
Drugs targeting type I IFN receptor or IFNs themselves are in clinical use or trials.
Other therapies target T-cell responses (IL-17, IL-23, IL-12/23, IFN-$ \\$gamma $) and B-cell/plasma cell responses (anti-CD19, anti-CD20, anti-BLys).
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 3)

items.title: Therapeutic Advances in Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: Advances in autoimmune disease treatment have been driven by targeting cytokines and immune cell responses. This includes TNF blockers, JAK inhibitors, and therapies against B-cell and plasma cell abnormalities.
keyPoints: TNF blockade is effective for RA, psoriasis, seronegative spondyloarthropathies, and IBD.
JAK inhibitors are used for RA, psoriatic arthritis, IBD, SLE, primary Sjögren syndrome, vitiligo, and alopecia areata.
Type I IFN targeting therapies are in clinical use or trials.
T-cell response targeting drugs (IL-17, IL-23, IL-12/23, IFN-$ \\$gamma $) are available or under development.
B-cell/plasma cell targeting therapies (anti-CD19, anti-CD20, anti-BLys) approved for SLE, RA, AAV; others in trials.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 3)

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.title: Role of Molecular Mimicry in Autoimmunity
items.content: Infections can trigger autoimmune diseases through molecular mimicry, where microbial antigens (Ags) resemble self-epitopes. This mechanism is exemplified by rheumatic fever, where antibodies against Streptococcus M-protein cross-react with heart and neuronal proteins.
keyPoints: Molecular mimicry involves structural similarities between microbial Ags and self-epitopes.
Rheumatic fever: Antibodies to Streptococcus M-protein can lead to carditis, valvular damage, or Sydenham's chorea.
Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) linked to SLE, MS, RA, celiac disease, and Sjögren syndrome through molecular mimicry and B cell reprogramming.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.title: Bystander Mechanisms in Autoimmune Disease Induction
items.content: Infections may enhance the immunogenicity of autoantigens through bystander mechanisms. This involves cytokine release and activation of autoreactive lymphocytes, potentially leading to autoimmune diseases.
keyPoints: Bystander mechanism mediated by cytokine release can activate autoreactive lymphocytes.
Animal models show links between infections like coxsackievirus B3 and autoimmune myocarditis.
SARS-CoV-2 has been associated with new-onset autoimmunity, including APS, MIS-C, Kawasaki disease, and Guillain-Barré syndrome.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.title: Hygiene Hypothesis in Autoimmunity
items.content: The hygiene hypothesis suggests that reduced exposure to infections in developed countries may increase the risk of autoimmune diseases by limiting immune regulatory pathways.
keyPoints: Parasites and microbes in developing countries can suppress immune responses, potentially reducing autoimmunity.
Higher prevalence of ADs in developed countries might be due to less microbial exposure.
Epidemiologic data supports this hypothesis; however, mechanisms remain under investigation.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE
items.question: Which statement best describes the difference between autoimmunity and autoimmune diseases?
options: Autoimmunity always leads to clinically relevant pathogenic responses.
Autoimmune diseases are characterized by harmful immune responses to self-antigens that result in clinical manifestations.
Autoimmunity is a condition where the immune system fails to recognize any antigens.
Autoimmune diseases only affect males and have no impact on females.
items.explanation: Autoimmune diseases involve harmful immune responses against self-antigens leading to clinical symptoms, unlike autoimmunity which may not always be pathogenic.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE
items.question: What is the estimated prevalence of autoimmune diseases in the United States as of 2009?
items.explanation: The estimated prevalence was approximately 7.6–9.4% of the U.S. population in 2009, equating to about 25–31 million people.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE
items.question: Which statement is true regarding the incidence of autoimmune diseases?
options: The incidence of autoimmune diseases has been decreasing over time.
There is no clear trend in the incidence of autoimmune diseases.
Autoimmune diseases are more prevalent with age and show an increasing trend in incidence.
Autoimmune diseases only affect individuals under 30 years old.
items.explanation: The prevalence of autoimmune diseases tends to increase with age, and there is a trend for increased incidence over time.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE

items.question: Which genetic factor is considered the best predictor of autoimmune disease (AD) risk?
items.explanation: HLA haplotype is considered the best predictor of AD risk.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · GENETIC AND EPIGENETIC FACTORS

items.question: What percentage range of disease concordance in monozygotic twins suggests a role for epigenetic factors in autoimmune diseases?
items.explanation: The disease concordance in monozygotic twins is between 15 and 30%, suggesting a role for epigenetic factors.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · GENETIC AND EPIGENETIC FACTORS

items.question: Which of the following genes has been associated with susceptibility to multiple autoimmune diseases including SLE, RA, and primary Sjögren syndrome?
items.explanation: The STAT4 gene has been associated with susceptibility to SLE, RA, and primary Sjögren syndrome.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · GENETIC AND EPIGENETIC FACTORS

items.question: Which of the following environmental exposures has been linked to an enhanced risk for developing clinical autoimmunity?
items.explanation: Ultraviolet light is mentioned as an environmental exposure linked to enhanced risk for developing clinical autoimmunity.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms may promote activation of innate and adaptive immune responses contributing to autoimmune disease development?
items.explanation: The synthesis of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is one mechanism by which environmental factors may promote activation of immune responses contributing to autoimmune disease development.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS

items.question: Which dietary factor has been associated with an increased risk for type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM)?
options: Higher gluten intake in early childhood
High intake of omega-3 fatty acids
items.explanation: Higher gluten intake in early childhood is associated with an increased risk for T1DM.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms is proposed to increase autoimmunity risk due to the presence of two X chromosomes?
options: Enhanced expression of X-linked immunity-related genes such as TLR-7, TLR-8, and IRAK-1.
Decreased expression of Y-linked immunity-related genes.
Increased production of type II interferons.
Reduction in the number of senescent immune cells.
items.explanation: Enhanced expression of X-linked immunity-related genes such as TLR-7, TLR-8, and IRAK-1 is proposed to increase autoimmunity risk due to the presence of two X chromosomes.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ☐ SEX | AGING

items.question: Which sex hormone has been shown to modulate neutrophil immunometabolism and maturation?
items.explanation: Estrogen is known to modulate neutrophil immunometabolism and maturation.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ☐ SEX | AGING

items.question: What effect do androgens have on B-cell and neutrophil maturation in SLE?
items.explanation: Androgens decrease B-cell and neutrophil maturation and can suppress autoantibody synthesis in SLE.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ☐ SEX | AGING

items.subtopic: ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE
items.question: Which Toll-like receptors (TLRs) are mentioned as potentially failing to distinguish between self- and microorganism-derived nucleic acids?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that certain TLRs (7–9) can sense nucleic acids and may not properly distinguish between self- and microorganism-derived nucleic acids.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE

items.subtopic: ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE
items.question: What is a consequence of delayed clearance of dead cells as described in the passage?
options: Reduced synthesis of type I IFN
Release of proinflammatory components and nucleic acids through secondary necrosis
items.explanation: The passage states that delayed clearance promotes the breakdown of apoptotic cells with release of proinflammatory components and nucleic acids to the extracellular space through secondary necrosis.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE

items.subtopic: ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE
items.question: Which type of cell death is associated with oxidation of nucleic acids by ROS or enzymes, enhancing their half-life?
items.explanation: The passage describes that during NETosis, oxidation of nucleic acids by ROS or enzymes can enhance the half-life of modified RNA and DNA.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.question: Which of the following autoimmune diseases (ADs) is NOT mentioned as being associated with dysregulation in the type I IFN pathway?
items.explanation: Multiple sclerosis is not mentioned as being associated with dysregulation in the type I IFN pathway.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.question: Which innate immune cell type is primarily responsible for producing large amounts of IFN-α and implicated in SLE pathogenesis?
items.explanation: Plasmacytoid DCs synthesize large amounts of IFN-α and are implicated in SLE pathogenesis.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.question: What is the role of neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) in autoimmune diseases?
options: They externalize modified autoantigens and induce inflammatory responses.
items.explanation: Dysregulated NET formation promotes the externalization of modified autoantigens and induces aberrant innate and tissue stromal inflammatory responses.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.question: Which concept describes an immune response that is faster and more robust to a second stimulus than the first?
items.explanation: Trained immunity describes immune responses that are faster and more robust to a second stimulus than the first.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.question: Which mechanism is NOT involved in peripheral tolerance to prevent autoreactivity?
options: Lymphocyte anergy due to lack of costimulation
Cell death and removal of autoreactive lymphocytes
items.explanation: Peripheral tolerance mechanisms include lymphocyte anergy, cell death, suppression by regulatory cells, but not increased frequency of lymphocyte precursors.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.question: Which type of autoantibodies are primarily found in healthy individuals?
items.explanation: In healthy individuals, natural autoantibodies are primarily IgM that are encoded by unmutated V(D)J genes and have a moderate affinity for self-antigens.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a mechanism through which autoAbs can be pathogenic?
options: Activation of inflammatory cascades via complement pathway
Opsonization of cells or soluble factors
items.explanation: AutoAbs can interfere with cell functions, activate inflammatory pathways, and opsonize cells but are not primarily involved in stimulating lymphocyte proliferation.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a central tolerance mechanism?
options: Lymphocyte anergy due to lack of costimulation
Deletion of autoreactive T cells in the thymus
items.explanation: Central tolerance involves the deletion of autoreactive T cells in the thymus and high-affinity autoreactive B cells in the bone marrow.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.question: Which autoantigen is targeted by autoAbs in Graves' disease?
items.explanation: In Graves' disease, autoAbs are directed against the thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) receptor on thyroid cells.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of Hashimoto's thyroiditis?
options: formation of autoAbs to thyroid peroxidase and thyroglobulin
items.explanation: Hashimoto's thyroiditis involves the formation of autoAbs to thyroid peroxidase and thyroglobulin.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.question: Which autoantibodies are specific for rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and can be detected years before diagnosis?
items.explanation: Anticitrullinated peptide antibodies (ACPAs) are specific for RA and can be detected in serum years before diagnosis.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.question: In primary biliary cholangitis, which autoantibodies are characteristic?
items.explanation: Primary biliary cholangitis is characterized by elevated levels of antimitochondrial antibodies.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.question: Which cytokine blockade is highly effective in a significant percentage of patients with RA, psoriasis, seronegative spondyloarthropathies, and IBD?
items.explanation: Blocking TNF is highly effective in significant percentages of patients with RA, psoriasis, seronegative spondyloarthropathies, and IBD.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 3)

items.question: Which type of inhibitors are used to downregulate downstream signaling pathways for type I IFNs in several autoimmune diseases?
items.explanation: Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors are used to downregulate downstream signaling pathways for type I IFNs in several autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following therapies targets abnormal B-cell and plasma cell responses?
options: Type I IFN receptor targeting drugs
items.explanation: Therapies that target abnormal B-cell and plasma cell responses include anti-CD19, anti-CD20, and anti-BLys.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 3)

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.question: Which virus has been linked to multiple autoimmune diseases including SLE, MS, RA, celiac disease, and Sjögren syndrome through mechanisms such as molecular mimicry?
items.explanation: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) has been linked to several autoimmune diseases through molecular mimicry and other mechanisms.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.question: Which mechanism involves the release of cytokines that activate autoreactive lymphocytes during infections?
items.explanation: The bystander mechanism involves cytokine release and activation of autoreactive lymphocytes.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.question: What is the role of molecular mimicry in rheumatic fever?
options: Abs to M-protein cross-react with heart and neuronal proteins
items.explanation: In rheumatic fever, antibodies to the M-protein of group A Streptococcus cross-react with proteins in the heart and brain.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.question: Which autoimmune condition is associated with SARS-CoV-2 infection?
items.explanation: SARS-CoV-2 infection has been linked to Guillain-Barré syndrome among other autoimmune conditions.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE
items.statement: Autoimmune diseases are among the top 10 leading causes of death in women.
items.explanation: As a group, autoimmune diseases rank within the top 10 leading causes of death for women.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE

items.subtopic: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE
items.statement: Individuals with one autoimmune disease have no increased risk of developing another.
items.explanation: An individual affected by an autoimmune disease has a high risk of developing another autoimmune condition.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE

items.statement: Autoimmune diseases are always monogenic.
items.explanation: Most autoimmune diseases are polygenic, although some monogenic disorders have been described.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · GENETIC AND EPIGENETIC FACTORS

items.statement: Epigenetic factors can influence the early onset of certain autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: Epigenetic factors have been implicated in the early onset of some autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · GENETIC AND EPIGENETIC FACTORS

items.statement: Cigarette smoking has been hypothesized to promote citrullination and carbamylation of proteins, contributing to autoimmune disease development.
items.explanation: The passage states that cigarette smoking promotes citrullination and carbamylation, which can contribute to the development of autoimmune diseases in genetically predisposed individuals.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS

items.statement: Deficiency of vitamin D is associated with a decreased risk for autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that deficiency of vitamin D is associated with an enhanced risk, not decreased risk, for autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS

items.statement: Aging leads to a reduction in proinflammatory features of immune cells.
items.explanation: Aging leads to accumulation of senescent innate and adaptive immune cells with proinflammatory features, known as inflammaging.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ☐ SEX | AGING

items.statement: Estrogen can activate B- and T-cell responses.
items.explanation: Estrogen activates B- and T-cell responses and promotes antibody production.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ☐ SEX | AGING

items.subtopic: ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE
items.statement: Deficiencies in complement pathway components are associated with a decreased frequency of SLE.
items.explanation: The passage states there is a high frequency of SLE in patients with deficiencies of complement pathway components, implying an association with increased frequency.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE

items.subtopic: ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE
items.statement: Genetic predisposition can impair mechanisms that usually contain the risk of responding to self-nucleic acids.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that in genetically predisposed individuals, these containment mechanisms may be impaired.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ABERRANT CELL DEATH AND CELL DEATH CLEARANCE

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.statement: Monocytes, macrophages, and NK cells show evidence of activation and dysregulation in autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: A causal link between the dysregulation of monocytes and macrophages and the development of AD has been proposed.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.statement: Plasmacytoid DCs are primarily involved in antigen presentation rather than type I IFN production.
items.explanation: Plasmacytoid DCs are major producers of type I IFNs, not primarily involved in antigen presentation.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.statement: High-affinity, somatically mutated IgG autoAbs are typically found in healthy individuals.
items.explanation: High-affinity, somatically mutated IgG autoAbs are associated with autoimmune diseases and not typically found in healthy individuals.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.statement: Autoantibodies can serve as diagnostic tools or markers of disease activity.
items.explanation: AutoAbs are used in the diagnosis and assessment of disease severity in various autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.statement: AutoAbs to multiple targets in T1DM indicate a higher risk of evolving into clinically apparent disease.
items.explanation: Individuals who develop Abs to multiple targets have a higher risk of progressing to clinically apparent T1DM.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.statement: Antimitochondrial antibodies in primary biliary cholangitis are confirmed to have a pathogenic role.
items.explanation: Whether antimitochondrial antibodies have a pathogenic role in primary biliary cholangitis remains unclear.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.statement: Type I IFNs are not considered major players in autoimmunity.
items.explanation: Identifying type I IFNs as major players in autoimmunity has led to the development of strategies targeting their downstream signaling pathways.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 3)

items.statement: Drugs that target IL-17, IL-23, and IL-12/23 are being developed for autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: Other drugs that target aberrant T-cell responses and their downstream effects, including IL-17, IL-23, and IL-12/23, are available or under development.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 3)

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.statement: The hygiene hypothesis suggests that exposure to parasites may increase the risk of autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: The hygiene hypothesis suggests that reduced exposure to parasites and microbes in developed countries might increase the risk for autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.statement: Paraneoplastic syndromes can occur before the diagnosis of an underlying neoplasm.
items.explanation: Paraneoplastic syndromes often present with autoimmune responses prior to the detection of cancer.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE
items.assertion: The immune system should be able to distinguish self from nonself.
items.reason: This ability helps avoid harmful responses to the body's own tissues.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why distinguishing self from nonself is crucial.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases | DEFINITION, INCIDENCE, AND PREVALENCE

items.assertion: The presence of two X chromosomes is associated with a higher incidence of autoimmunity.
items.reason: This is due to enhanced expression of X-linked immunity-related genes like TLR-7, which promotes synthesis of type I interferons.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · ☐ SEX | AGING

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.assertion: Neutrophils are implicated in the pathogenesis of several autoimmune diseases.
items.reason: Certain proinflammatory subsets of neutrophils and dysregulation in mechanisms of neutrophil cell death contribute to disease pathogenesis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS
items.assertion: Trained immunity can lead to beneficial outcomes in infections.
items.reason: It involves changes in the epigenetic landscape of innate immune cells that enhance their response.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · DYSREGULATION OF INNATE IMMUNE CELLS AND INNATE IMMUNE PATHWAYS

items.assertion: Tregs play a crucial role in maintaining immune tolerance.
items.reason: Abnormalities in Tregs can amplify autoimmunity in diseases like SLE, RA, and IBD.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Tregs are crucial for maintaining immune tolerance.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.assertion: Central tolerance mechanisms involve lymphocyte anergy.
items.reason: Lymphocyte anergy is a peripheral tolerance mechanism.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because central tolerance involves deletion of autoreactive cells, not anergy. The reason is true as it correctly identifies anergy as a peripheral tolerance mechanism.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 1)

items.assertion: Age-associated B cells (ABCs) play a pathogenic role in several autoimmune diseases.
items.reason: ABCs produce cytokines and autoAbs, contributing to disease pathology.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.assertion: Regulatory B cells (Bregs) promote tissue pathology in autoimmune diseases.
items.reason: Dysfunction of Bregs may be implicated in autoimmunity predisposition and propagation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; Bregs suppress immune responses, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 2)

items.assertion: Blocking TNF is effective in treating several autoimmune diseases.
items.reason: TNF plays a role in the development and perpetuation of various autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why blocking TNF is effective.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · LYMPHOCYTE AUTOREACTIVITY (part 3)

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.assertion: The eye is considered an immune-privileged site.
items.reason: This status helps prevent vision loss from inflammatory injury.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the eye's immune privilege prevents inflammation-related damage.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY

items.subtopic: INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
items.assertion: Gastrointestinal dysbiosis is implicated in the development of RA.
items.reason: Microbial superantigens stimulate autoreactive lymphocytes through antigen specificity.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because microbial superantigens act independently of antigen specificity.
items.reference: Autoimmunity and Autoimmune Diseases · INFECTIONS AND AUTOIMMUNITY | PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES, CANCER, AND AUTOIMMUNITY
